title: How to Make Spiralized Vegetable Soup for a Comforting Meal
meta_description: Learn how to make a comforting spiralized vegetable soup using fresh veggies and a handy vegetable spiralizer. Simple steps, tasty broth, and low‑carb noodles a
html:
When the weather turns chilly, a bowl of hot soup can feel like a warm hug. Using a vegetable spiralizer to turn zucchini, carrots, and even sweet potatoes into noodle‑like strands adds a comforting texture while keeping the dish low‑carb. Below is a step‑by‑step guide to creating a hearty spiralized vegetable soup that’s perfect for weeknight dinners or cozy weekend brunches.

Why Choose Spiralized Vegetable Soup?

Spiralized vegetable soup combines the soothing qualities of a classic broth with the light, fresh feel of veggie noodles. The spiralized strands soak up flavor without becoming mushy, so each bite stays vibrant. It’s also a great way to sneak extra vegetables into your diet, and the low‑carb profile makes it friendly for those watching carbs.

Essential Tools and Ingredients

Before you start, gather these basics. Having everything ready will keep the cooking process smooth and enjoyable.

  • Vegetable Spiralizer (handheld or countertop) – ideal for turning zucchini, carrots, and other veggies into noodles.
  • 2 medium zucchini, washed and trimmed
  • 2 large carrots, peeled
  • 1 sweet potato, peeled (optional for extra heartiness)
  • 1 onion, diced
  • 2 cloves garlic, minced
  • 4 cups low‑sodium chicken or vegetable broth
  • 1 can (14 oz) diced tomatoes, undrained
  • 1 tsp dried thyme
  • 1 tsp dried oregano
  • Salt and freshly cracked black pepper to taste
  • 2 tbsp olive oil
  • Fresh parsley or basil for garnish

Step 1: Spiralize Your Vegetables

Start by attaching the appropriate blade on your vegetable spiralizer. For zucchini and carrots, a medium‑sized blade works best. Feed each vegetable through the hand‑held or countertop device, collecting the strands in a bowl. If you’re using a sweet potato, a larger blade may be needed to handle the denser texture. Once spiraled, set the noodles aside—they’ll add texture right before serving.

Step 2: Build the Flavor Base

Heat the olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add the diced onion and sauté for 3–4 minutes until it becomes translucent. Stir in the minced garlic and cook for another 30 seconds; this releases its aroma without burning.

Next, pour in the broth and diced tomatoes. Bring the mixture to a gentle boil, then reduce the heat to a simmer. Add the dried thyme, oregano, salt, and pepper. Let the broth simmer for about 10 minutes so the flavors meld.

Step 3: Cook the Harder Veggies

If you’re using sweet potato, add the cubed pieces now. Simmer for 8–10 minutes, or until the sweet potato is just tender. This timing ensures the sweet potato softens without turning mushy, preserving the bite of the spiralized noodles added later.

Step 4: Add the Spiralized Noodles

When the broth is flavorful and the sweet potato is nearly done, stir in the zucchini and carrot noodles. Because they’re already “cooked” by the spiralizer, they only need a quick 2–3 minute warm‑up. Overcooking will cause them to release excess water and lose their pleasant bite.

Give the soup a final taste and adjust seasoning if needed. A splash of lemon juice can brighten the soup if you prefer a slightly tangy note.

Step 5: Serve and Garnish

Ladle the soup into bowls, then sprinkle fresh parsley or basil on top for color and fresh flavor. For an extra touch of richness, drizzle a tiny drizzle of olive oil or add a sprinkle of grated Parmesan cheese—optional but delightful.

This spiralized vegetable soup stays warm for about an hour, but the noodle strands are best enjoyed while still slightly firm. If you have leftovers, store the broth and vegetables separately from the noodles to prevent sogginess.

Tips for Customizing Your Soup

Feel free to experiment with different veggies and proteins. Here are a few ideas:

  • Protein boost: Add shredded chicken, cooked shrimp, or canned white beans during the last 5 minutes of cooking.
  • Spice it up: A pinch of red pepper flakes or a dash of hot sauce can give the soup a gentle heat.
  • Herb variations: Swap thyme and oregano for rosemary or sage for a different aromatic profile.
  • Creamier texture: Stir in a splash of coconut milk or heavy cream at the end for a silkier mouthfeel.

FAQ

Can I use a regular vegetable peeler instead of a spiralizer?

Yes, a standard peeler will create thin ribbons, but a vegetable spiralizer yields longer, noodle‑like strands that hold broth better and provide a more satisfying texture.

How long can I store the soup in the refrigerator?

The broth and cooked vegetables keep well for up to 3 days. Store the spiralized noodles separately and combine them just before reheating to maintain their bite.

Is this soup suitable for a gluten‑free diet?

Absolutely. All the ingredients—vegetables, broth, and spices—are naturally gluten‑free. Just double‑check that your broth brand doesn’t contain hidden wheat additives.
